Q1: I organized my study routine around listening rather than reading, honestly. I'd set aside blocks of time, usually around 31 hours a week, where I'd play lecture recordings or audio materials while I was doing other things or just sitting quietly. I didn't always attend classes in person because I found I could absorb the material better through recordings at my own pace. My routine was pretty consistent - I'd dedicate mornings to listening and evenings to working through assignments.
Q2: My pattern was fairly regular, actually. I'm someone who likes consistency even when my motivation isn't always high. Deadlines would make me intensify my efforts, but I generally maintained a steady rhythm throughout the semester. Having extracurricular activities meant I needed structure, so I'd block out specific times regardless of what was due.
Q3: Mainly I logged in to check on assignment deadlines and download materials I could use offline. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were new announcements or if anything had changed with the course schedule. To be honest, I wasn't someone who browsed around the LMS much - I went in with a purpose, got what I needed, and logged out.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I checked habitually. I'd remember to log in when I needed something specific, but it wasn't like checking my phone or anything automatic. I think because I prefer auditory learning, the LMS felt more like a tool to access materials than a place to spend time in.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful for me - I watched over 265 minutes worth because I could listen and re-listen to explanations. Audio and video content suited my learning style perfectly. I'd often play them multiple times, focusing on different aspects each time. The materials I could download and convert to audio or listen to repeatedly were my lifeline in this course.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion forums. I never posted anything there because I just don't engage well through writing - I process information through listening and talking, not typing responses. Reading through text-heavy resources also felt like a chore, so I'd often skip supplementary readings unless they were absolutely essential for assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually hear someone explaining concepts rather than just reading about them. If it helped me understand something I'd been struggling with, that made it valuable. I also valued anything that directly connected to my assignments since I was focused on completing those well.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford mentally. With my anxiety being what it is, I focused heavily on required work to make sure I hit those targets. Optional stuff would trigger this worry about whether I was missing something important, but I'd usually convince myself to let it go and focus on what was mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely, this was huge for me. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while walking or doing chores. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos on similar topics because hearing different explanations helped concepts click. A lot of my actual learning happened away from my computer entirely.
Q10: Most of my studying probably wouldn't show up in the LMS data. I'd listen to downloaded lectures repeatedly, discuss concepts with peers face-to-face, and work through problems while listening to recordings I'd saved. My 36 peer interactions were mostly in person or through messaging apps, not through the platform itself.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video once, download it or take detailed audio notes, and then never open it again on the LMS. My engagement with that material continued for days or weeks afterward, but the system would only see that single access. This was my standard approach for almost everything.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety spiked and everything felt overwhelming. Even though I'm generally resilient and can bounce back, those anxious periods made it hard to even open the LMS. I'd feel this pressure building and sometimes just had to step away and do some mindfulness exercises before I could re-engage.
Q13: My low attendance was partly because of competing responsibilities with my extracurricular activities. When I felt fatigued or anxious, I'd prioritize working offline where I felt more in control. The LMS sometimes felt like this overwhelming space with too many things demanding attention, so I'd retreat to my simpler offline study methods.
Q14: Sometimes I'd be watching videos but my mind would be elsewhere, especially during high-anxiety periods. I'd have the material playing but I wasn't really processing it - just going through the motions. Those sessions felt wasted even though they'd register as engagement time in the system.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts indefinitely - that's why I have zero. Writing in forums triggers my anxiety more than other activities, and I kept telling myself I'd do it later but never did. I knew participation might help my grade, but the anxiety around it was too much, so I focused my energy on assignments instead.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver for logging into the LMS. I'd check in more frequently as due dates approached, making sure I had everything I needed. My assignment completion rate was high because I took deadlines seriously, but my engagement between deadlines was pretty minimal on the platform itself.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me, though it often came with a wave of anxiety before I could process it constructively. Once I calmed down, good feedback motivated me to maintain my approach, while critical feedback pushed me to adjust. I appreciated feedback that was specific because it gave me clear direction.
Q18: The course structure with its clear deadlines helped me plan my offline study time effectively. Having 12 online courses meant I needed to be strategic about where I spent my energy. The structure gave me enough flexibility to learn in my preferred auditory way while still meeting requirements.
Q19: The availability of video content made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. What made it harder was the emphasis on written discussion participation - that felt like it was designed for a different type of learner. More audio-based interaction options would have helped me engage more fully.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding without finding it, clicking through materials trying to make sense of things but not being able to translate that into completed work. Others might be dealing with anxiety or perfectionism that makes them consume content but freeze when it comes to producing something. The activity shows effort, just not productive output.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My exam score was decent and I got a Merit, but my LMS engagement level was low. I did most of my real learning offline through audio materials and peer discussions. The LMS data would suggest I wasn't engaged, but I was just engaged in ways the system couldn't track.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and completed tasks - the concrete actions. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline learning, the conversations with classmates, and the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They especially miss auditory learners like me who download once and study repeatedly offline.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively trying to understand and connect with the material, not just checking boxes. For me, it's about listening deeply, asking questions in my head, discussing with peers, and applying what I learn. It's a mental state more than a measurable activity - I can be deeply engaged while appearing inactive online.
Q24: My mindfulness practice that helps me manage anxiety and stay resilient wouldn't show up at all. Neither would the hours I spend listening to downloaded lectures, my conversations with the 36 peers I interacted with outside the platform, or the genuine happiness I feel when concepts finally click. The emotional and offline dimensions of my learning are completely invisible to the data.
